content.full_text:
FAIR p OLITICAL p RACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 958 I 4-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Mr. John R. Strout 
Ms. Heidi K. Abegg 
Webster, Chamberlain & Bean, LLP 
1747 Pennsylvania Avenue, N.W. 
Washington, D.C. 20006 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-11-143 
Dear Mr. Strout and Ms. Abegg: 
September 15, 2011 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Institute for Justice 
regarding campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
FACTS 
You are representing the Institute for Justice, a section 501(c)(3) nonprofit corporation 
organized in the District of Columbia and located in the Commonwealth of Virginia. The 
Institute for Justice ("IJ") is a civil liberties public interest law firm that engages in litigation and 
advocacy in the courts of law and in the court of public opinion. Thus, IJ exists primarily for 
purposes other than making contributions or expenditures for political purposes and is a "multi­
purpose" organization. IJ itself is funded primarily through donations by like-minded 
individuals and foundations. 
IJ would like to provide assistance in the form of services to a prospective local 
California ballot measure group (such ballot measure has not yet qualified for the ballot). 
Specifically, IJ intends to provide legal assistance and on-the-ground assistance with organizing 
rallies and events. In addition, IJ employees may review press releases, help organize the local 
ballot measure group's calendar, give "pep" talks, and make introductions and connections with 
those who have organized similar ballot measure groups in other states. IJ may also pay for 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-11-143 
Page No. 2 
printing and copying costs of the attachments to the petitions and the printing costs of flyers and 
t-shirts. IJ may also pay for the legal fees of a California attorney who will assist with the 
petition process. IJ will not engage in any signature gathering. To provide some of these 
services, employees of IJ will need to travel to California from IJ's offices in Virginia or 
elsewhere, and therefore, IJ will incur travel costs and employees will spend time traveling to 
assist the local ballot measure group. IJ estimates that the value of the assistance provided will 
total at least $5,000 and is unlikely to total more than $25,000. 
You state that Uhas not yet qualified as a recipient committee under Section 82013(a) 
because it has not received contributions totaling $1,000 or more, made independent 
expenditures totaling $1,000 or more, or made contributions totaling $10,000 or more in 2011 or 
in any of the immediately preceding four calendar years. 
APPLICABLE LAW 
Disclosure of Nonprofit Spending in California Elections. 
Some organizations that receive donations or other payments (e.g., membership dues) for 
purposes other than making political expenditures in California nevertheless do, at times, make 
political expenditures to support or oppose California state or local candidates or ballot 
measures. These organizations are subject to the same disclosure rules as those that raise funds 
specifically for California elections in that they must disclose the sources of funds used for their 
political expenditures. Common examples are nonprofit organizations, federal and out-of-state 
political action committees, and local clubs that generally focus on educational and social 
activities. 
Section 82013 of the Act defines a "committee" as follows: 
'"Committee' means any person or combination of persons who directly or 
indirectly does any of the following: 
(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in 
a calendar year; 
(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) 
or more in a calendar year; or 
(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more in a 
calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees. 
A person or combination of persons that becomes a committee shall retain 
its status as a committee until such time as that status is terminated pursuant to 
Section 84214." 
' 
1 
Regulation 18215 sets out a rule for these multipurpose organizations to use for 
determining if they qualify as a recipient committee required to disclose funding sources under 
the Act. A multipurpose organization is receiving "contributions" if its members or donors make 
payments to the organization (including dues) and, at the time of making the payments, the 


File No. A-11-143 
Page No. 3 
donors know or have reason to know that the funds, with which their payments will be 
commingled, will be used to make contributions or expenditures to support or oppose California 
state or local candidates or ballot measures. There is a presumption that the donors do not have 
reason to know that all or part of their payments made to the multi-purpose organization will be 
used for political activities in California unless the organization has made expenditures or 
contributions totaling at least $1,000 during the current calendar year or any of the immediately 
preceding four calendar years. 
Regulation 18215 provides as follows: 
"(b) The term 'contribution' includes: 
(1) Any payment made to a person or organization other than a candidate 
or committee, when, at the time of making the payment, the donor knows or has 
reason to know that the payment, or funds with which the payment will be 
commingled, will be used to make contributions or expenditures. If the donor 
knows or has reason to know that only part of the payment will be used to make 
contributions or expenditures, the payment shall be apportioned on a reasonable 
basis in order to determine the amount of the contribution. 
There shall be a presumption that the donor does not have reason to know 
that all or part of the payment will be used to make expenditures or contributions, 
unless the person or organization has made expenditures or contributions of at 
least one thousand dollars ($1,000) in the aggregate during the calendar year in 
which the payment occurs, or any of the immediately preceding four calendar 
years." 
Regulation 18215(b)(l) is sometimes colloquially referred to as the "first-bite of the 
apple" rule. Under this rule, the first time an organization uses dues or other receipts to make 
contributions or expenditures, the organization's members or donors are presumed to have 
gained knowledge that the funds they donate in the future may be used for political purposes. 
This is the "first bite." 
Accordingly, once the organization has made its first political contributions or 
expenditures totaling $1,000 or more, the presumption that donors to such organizations do not 
have reason to know that the payment would be used to make contributions or expenditures no 
longer applies. For the remainder of that year and the following four years, any donor to the 
organization presumably knows or has reason to know that funds with which the donations will 
be commingled may be used for political purposes in California. If a subsequent contribution or 
expenditure of $1,000 or i;nore is maqe by the organization during the applicable period (current 
year plus four following years), the organization becomes a recipient committee, and any 
donations or membership fees it receives after the "first bite" contribution has been taken or 
expenditures of $1,000 or more have been made, are deemed to have been received for political 
purposes, and the sources of any funds used by the organizations to make those contributions 
would subject to disclosure on a reasonably apportioned basis. 


File No. A-11-143 
Page No. 4 
Under the Act, a nonprofit that is actively contributing to or spending on California 
candidates or ballot measures, which qualifies as a committee under Section 82013 and 
Regulation 18215(b)(l), must file disclosure reports in California showing the nonprofit's 
sources of funds equal to the amount of its political activity in California. For example, if a 
nonprofit organization spends $2,000 supporting a ballot measure in California and then spends 
another $25,000 on the ballot measure, the nonprofit organization qualifies as a committee under 
the Act and must disclose $25,000 in contributions. 
Under California law, the required disclosure of the nonprofit organization's donors is 
limited to the amount of the organization's political activity in California. The United States 
Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit upheld California's tailored disclosure of nonprofit 
spending in California's elections in a thorough discussion in California ProLife Council, Inc. v. 
Randolph, et al., 507 F.3d 1172 (9th Cir. 2007). The court upheld the presumption in Regulation 
18215(b)(l), concluding: " ... the presumption in§ 18215(b)(l) is narrowly tailored .... 
Moreover, as interpreted by California,§ 18215(b)(l) does not burden substantially more speech 
than necessary and serves to advance the compelling informational interest." Id. at 38. 
Your questions also ask how to identify the sources of funds of the nonprofit' s spending. 
With respect to identifying the donors, under Section 18215(b)(l), if only a part of a donor's 
payment will be used to make contributions or expenditures, the payment shall be apportioned 
for reporting purposes. The Act does not currently provide a specific accounting method for 
determining the amount of each donor's or member's payment that must be reported as a 
contribution. Under current advice, any reasonable allocation method is appropriate. The 
Commission is planning to consider at its December 2011 meeting a regulation on identifying the 
funding sources for nonprofit spending in California elections. 
QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS 
1. After IJ has taken its one bite of the apple, and has then spent or made an additional 
$1,000 in in-kind contributions, who must IJ report as the source of funds used for political 
purposes: ( a) itself or (b) its own donors? 
After IJ has made its first contribution or expenditure to support or oppose a local 
California ballot measure, IJ' s donors are presumed to be on notice that the organization is 
participating in California elections. (Regulation 18215(b)(l).) When IJ has then spent or made 
an additional $1,000 in in-kind contributions, it must report donors of contributions made after 
the date of IJ's first contribution or expenditure on the California measure as the source of funds 
used for political purposes. It will report contributors of amounts over $100 as the source of 
funds used for political purposes. 


,File No. A-11-143 
Page No. 5 
Does the answer to this question change if, similar to the Rehrig Advice Letter, No. A-07-
126, IJ does not specifically raise any funds to support its efforts in California., and only uses 
general ftmds on hand prior to its taking its first bite of the apple? 
I 
Under Regulation 18215(b)(l), IJ's first contribution to or expenditure on the California 
ballot measure puts IJ's donors on notice of its political activity. If IJ has received donations 
after its first contribution to or expenditure on the California measure, it must report those donors 
as the sources of funds for its political activity in California. If, however, IJ h(ls not received any 
donations after its first contribution to or expenditure on the California measure, and it is using 
general funds on hand that it received prior to this, then IJ need not identify donors who were not 
on notice of IJ' s political activity in California. In this case, IJ may identify itself as the source 
of the funds. 
Does the answer to this question change if the funds are raised after taking its first bite of 
the apple and are earmarked for non-California purposes? 
When IJ is identifying the sources of funds used for its political expenGJitures in 
California, it should not report donations to IJ that are earmarked for non-California purposes. 
2. If JJ must disclose its own donors as the source of funds used for political purposes, 
after IJ has taken its first bite at the apple, could IJ alternatively raise money from a select 
number of donors specifically for its California effort, and disclose only those donors as the 
source of funds used for political purposes ( assuming that such funds raised equal or exceed the 
amount of money spent for political purposes)? 
Yes, if a multipurpose organization solicits funds for political purposes, the organization 
must disclose the full amount of each earmarked contribution. IJ may raise money from a select 
number of donors specifically for its California effort, and disclose only those donors as the 
source of funds used for political purposes, to the extent that the funds raised equal or exceed the 
amount of money spent for political purposes. If the amounts IJ raises specifically for its 
California effort exceed the amount of money IJ spends on the measure, the excess funds may 
not be moved back to IJ's general treasury, but must be spent according to the Act's use-of-funds 
rules (Sections 89510-89518). 
Would those donors have reporting requirements as major donors if they give IJ more 
than $10,000, and/or will those donors have to be disclosed as sponsors of the local ballot 
measure committee? 
If a contributor to IJ gives more than $10,000 specifically to support a California ballot 
measure, the contributor will be required to file a major donor report. (Section 82013(c).) 
Regarding sponsored committees, Section 82048.7 provides: 
"(b) A person sponsors a committee if any of the following apply: 


File No. A-11-143 
Page No. 6 
( 1) The committee receives 80 percent or more of its contributions from 
the person or its members, officers, employees, or shareholders. 
(2) The person collects contributions for the committee by use of payroll 
deductions or dues from its members, officers, or employees. 
(3) The person, alone or in combination with other organizations, provides 
all or nearly all of the administrative services for the committee. 
(4) The person, alone or in combination with other organizations, sets the 
policies for soliciting contributions or making expenditures of committee 
funds." 
In addition, the name of a sponsored committee shall include the name of its sponsor. 
(Regulation 18419(b)(l).) If a donor has made contributions earmarked for the California ballot 
measure and IJ is determining how those contributions are spent, the donor will not be listed as a 
sponsor of the ballot measure committee, but IJ may qualify as a sponsor of the ballot measure 
committee if it meets any of the sponsorship criteria listed above. 
In addition, under the advertisement disclosure provisions of the Act, the top two donors 
of $50,000 or more to a ballot measure must be identified in the ballot measure committee's 
advertisements and the committee's name must include the economic or other special interest of 
its major donors of $50,000 or more. (Section 84503 and 84504.) IJ might qualify as a top 
donor to the ballot measure required to be disclosed under these sections, though you state that IJ 
currently does not anticipate spending more than $25,000 on the measure. 
3. Do expenses for in-kind assistance incurred prior to the petition/referendum 
qualifying for the ballot count for purposes of determining whether IJ has taken its "one bite of 
the apple" under Regulation 182 J S(b )( 1)? 
Yes, expenses for in-kind assistance to the local ballot measure effort in California 
incurred prior to the petition/referendum qualifying for the ballot do count for purposes of 
determining whether IJ has taken its "first bite of the apple" under Regulation 18215(b)(l). 
4. Are travel time and expenses incurred by an employee traveling to California from 
Virginia or any other location to provide on-the-ground assistance considered in-kind 
contributions to the local ballot measure committee? If so, can the salary and travel expenses be 
prorated if there are also non-political purposes for the trip? 
Yes, travel time and expenses incurred by an employee traveling to California from 
Virginia or any other location to provide on-the-ground1assistance to a local ballot measure effort 
in California are considered in-kind contributions to the local ballot measure committee. The 
salary and travel expenses may be prorated if there are also non-political purposes for the trip. 


File No. A-11-143 
Page No. 7 
5. If the employee spends more than 10% of his or her compensated time in one month 
rendering services for political purposes, but not in a subsequent month, are the services in the 
subsequent month considered an in-kind contribution? 
No, under Regulation 18423, the employee must spend more than 10% of his or her 
compensated time in the subsequent month rendering personal services for political purposes 
before the pro-rata portion of his or her gross salary, reimbursement, or compensation is counted 
as a contribution or expenditure. 
6. If at the close of a reporting period, it is detennined that IJ' s in-kind contributions fell 
below 80% of the total contributions, can the ballot measure committee amend its statement of 
organization and delete IJ as a sponsor? 
Under Section 82048.7 discussed above, a person sponsors a committee if the "committee 
receives 80 percent of more of its contributions from the person or its members, officers, 
employees, or shareholders." If at the close of a reporting period, it is determined that !J's in­
kind contributions fell below 80 percent of the total contributions and IJ does not qualify as a 
sponsor under another criteria, the ballot measure committee may amend its statement of 
organization and delete IJ as a sponsor. (Olson Advice Letter, No. 1-92-604.) 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
HPW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
By: 
Hyla P. Wagner 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

organized in the District of Columbia and located in the Commonwealth of Virginia. The 
Institute for Justice ("IJ") is a civil liberties public interest law firm that engages in litigation and 
advocacy in the courts of law and in the court of public opinion. Thus, IJ exists primarily for

individuals and foundations.

California ballot measure group (such ballot measure has not yet qualified for the ballot).

rallies and events. In addition, IJ employees may review press releases, help organize the local 
ballot measure group's calendar, give "pep" talks, and make introductions and connections with 
those who have organized similar ballot measure groups in other states. IJ may also pay forprinting and copying costs of the attachments to the petitions and the printing costs of flyers and 
t-shirts. IJ may also pay for the legal fees of a California attorney who will assist with the 
petition process. IJ will not engage in any signature gathering. To provide some of these

elsewhere, and therefore, IJ will incur travel costs and employees will spend time traveling to 
assist the local ballot measure group. IJ estimates that the value of the assistance provided will

You state that Uhas not yet qualified as a recipient committee under Section 82013(a) 
because it has not received contributions totaling $1,000 or more, made independent

or more in a calendar year; or

calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees.

"(b) The term 'contribution' includes: 
(1) Any payment made to a person or organization other than a candidate 
or committee, when, at the time of making the payment, the donor knows or has 
reason to know that the payment, or funds with which the payment will be

knows or has reason to know that only part of the payment will be used to make

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: S AND ANSWERS 
1. After IJ has taken its one bite of the apple, and has then spent or made an additional 
$1,000 in in-kind contributions, who must IJ report as the source of funds used for political 
purposes: ( a) itself or (b) its own donors? 
After IJ has made its first contribution or expenditure to support or oppose a local 
California ballot measure, IJ' s donors are presumed to be on notice that the organization is 
participating in California elections. (Regulation 18215(b)(l).) When IJ has then spent or made 
an additional $1,000 in in-kind contributions, it must report donors of contributions made after 
the date of IJ's first contribution or expenditure on the California measure as the source of funds 
used for political purposes. It will report contributors of amounts over $100 as the source of 
funds used for political purposes. 


,Page No. 5 
Does the answer to this question change if, similar to the Rehrig Advice Letter, No. A-07-
126, IJ does not specifically raise any funds to support its efforts in California., and only uses 
general ftmds on hand prior to its taking its first bite of the apple? 
I 
Under Regulation 18215(b)(l), IJ's first contribution to or expenditure on the California 
ballot measure puts IJ's donors on notice of its political activity. If IJ has received donations 
after its first contribution to or expenditure on the California measure, it must report those donors 
as the sources of funds for its political activity in California. If, however, IJ h(ls not received any 
donations after its first contribution to or expenditure on the California measure, and it is using 
general funds on hand that it received prior to this, then IJ need not identify donors who were not 
on notice of IJ' s political activity in California. In this case, IJ may identify itself as the source 
of the funds. 
Does the answer to this question change if the funds are raised after taking its first bite of 
the apple and are earmarked for non-California purposes? 
When IJ is identifying the sources of funds used for its political expenGJitures in 
California, it should not report donations to IJ that are earmarked for non-California purposes. 
2. If JJ must disclose its own donors as the source of funds used for political purposes, 
after IJ has taken its first bite at the apple, could IJ alternatively raise money from a select 
number of donors specifically for its California effort, and disclose only those donors as the 
source of funds used for political purposes ( assuming that such funds raised equal or exceed the 
amount of money spent for political purposes)? 
Yes, if a multipurpose organization solicits funds for political purposes, the organization 
must disclose the full amount of each earmarked contribution. IJ may raise money from a select 
number of donors specifically for its California effort, and disclose only those donors as the 
source of funds used for political purposes, to the extent that the funds raised equal or exceed the 
amount of money spent for political purposes. If the amounts IJ raises specifically for its 
California effort exceed the amount of money IJ spends on the measure, the excess funds may 
not be moved back to IJ's general treasury, but must be spent according to the Act's use-of-funds 
rules (Sections 89510-89518). 
Would those donors have reporting requirements as major donors if they give IJ more 
than $10,000, and/or will those donors have to be disclosed as sponsors of the local ballot 
measure committee? 
If a contributor to IJ gives more than $10,000 specifically to support a California ballot 
measure, the contributor will be required to file a major donor report. (Section 82013(c).) 
Regarding sponsored committees, Section 82048.7 provides: 
"(b) A person sponsors a committee if any of the following apply:  
( 1) The committee receives 80 percent or more of its contributions from 
the person or its members, officers, employees, or shareholders. 
(2) The person collects contributions for the committee by use of payroll 
deductions or dues from its members, officers, or employees. 
(3) The person, alone or in combination with other organizations, provides 
all or nearly all of the administrative services for the committee. 
(4) The person, alone or in combination with other organizations, sets the 
policies for soliciting contributions or making expenditures of committee 
funds." 
In addition, the name of a sponsored committee shall include the name of its sponsor. 
(Regulation 18419(b)(l).) If a donor has made contributions earmarked for the California ballot 
measure and IJ is determining how those contributions are spent, the donor will not be listed as a 
sponsor of the ballot measure committee, but IJ may qualify as a sponsor of the ballot measure 
committee if it meets any of the sponsorship criteria listed above. 
In addition, under the advertisement disclosure provisions of the Act, the top two donors 
of $50,000 or more to a ballot measure must be identified in the ballot measure committee's 
advertisements and the committee's name must include the economic or other special interest of 
its major donors of $50,000 or more. (Section 84503 and 84504.) IJ might qualify as a top 
donor to the ballot measure required to be disclosed under these sections, though you state that IJ 
currently does not anticipate spending more than $25,000 on the measure. 
3. Do expenses for in-kind assistance incurred prior to the petition/referendum 
qualifying for the ballot count for purposes of determining whether IJ has taken its "one bite of 
the apple" under Regulation 182 J S(b )( 1)? 
Yes, expenses for in-kind assistance to the local ballot measure effort in California 
incurred prior to the petition/referendum qualifying for the ballot do count for purposes of 
determining whether IJ has taken its "first bite of the apple" under Regulation 18215(b)(l). 
4. Are travel time and expenses incurred by an employee traveling to California from 
Virginia or any other location to provide on-the-ground assistance considered in-kind 
contributions to the local ballot measure committee? If so, can the salary and travel expenses be 
prorated if there are also non-political purposes for the trip? 
Yes, travel time and expenses incurred by an employee traveling to California from 
Virginia or any other location to provide on-the-ground1assistance to a local ballot measure effort 
in California are considered in-kind contributions to the local ballot measure committee. The 
salary and travel expenses may be prorated if there are also non-political purposes for the trip.  
5. If the employee spends more than 10% of his or her compensated time in one month 
rendering services for political purposes, but not in a subsequent month, are the services in the 
subsequent month considered an in-kind contribution? 
No, under Regulation 18423, the employee must spend more than 10% of his or her 
compensated time in the subsequent month rendering personal services for political purposes 
before the pro-rata portion of his or her gross salary, reimbursement, or compensation is counted 
as a contribution or expenditure. 
6. If at the close of a reporting period, it is detennined that IJ' s in-kind contributions fell 
below 80% of the total contributions, can the ballot measure committee amend its statement of 
organization and delete IJ as a sponsor? 
Under Section 82048.7 discussed above, a person sponsors a committee if the "committee 
receives 80 percent of more of its contributions from the person or its members, officers, 
employees, or shareholders." If at the close of a reporting period, it is determined that !J's in­
kind contributions fell below 80 percent of the total contributions and IJ does not qualify as a 
sponsor under another criteria, the ballot measure committee may amend its statement of 
organization and delete IJ as a sponsor. (Olson Advice Letter, No. 1-92-604.)
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR p OLITICAL p RACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 958 I 4-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Mr. John R. Strout Ms. Heidi K. Abegg Webster, Chamberlain & Bean, LLP 1747 Pennsylvania Avenue, N.W. Washington, D.C. 20006 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-11-143 Dear Mr. Strout and Ms. Abegg: September 15, 2011 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Institute for Justice regarding campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 FACTS You are representing the Institute for Justice, a section 501(c)(3) nonprofit corporation organized in the District of Columbia and located in the Commonwealth of Virginia. The Institute for Justice ("IJ") is a civil liberties public interest law firm that engages in litigation and advocacy in the courts of law and in the court of public opinion. Thus, IJ exists primarily for purposes other than making contributions or expenditures for political purposes and is a "multi­ purpose" organization. IJ itself is funded primarily through donations by like-minded individuals and foundations. IJ would like to provide assistance in the form of services to a prospective local California ballot measure group (such ballot measure has not yet qualified for the ballot). Specifically, IJ intends to provide legal assistance and on-the-ground assistance with organizing rallies and events. In addition, IJ employees may review press releases, help organize the local ballot measure group's calendar, give "pep" talks, and make introductions and connections with those who have organized similar ballot measure groups in other states. IJ may also pay for 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-11-143 Page No. 2 printing and copying costs of the attachments to the petitions and the printing costs of flyers and t-shirts. IJ may also pay for the legal fees of a California attorney who will assist with the petition process. IJ will not engage in any signature gathering. To provide some of these services, employees of IJ will need to travel to California from IJ's offices in Virginia or elsewhere, and therefore, IJ will incur travel costs and employees will spend time traveling to assist the local ballot measure group. IJ estimates that the value of the assistance provided will total at least $5,000 and is unlikely to total more than $25,000. You state that Uhas not yet qualified as a recipient committee under Section 82013(a) because it has not received contributions totaling $1,000 or more, made independent expenditures totaling $1,000 or more, or made contributions totaling $10,000 or more in 2011 or in any of the immediately preceding four calendar years. APPLICABLE LAW Disclosure of Nonprofit
